content:
# Disagreement Log — Luke Chapter 23
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Date:** 2026-03-05

---

## Critical Disagreements

### Verse 34 — Prayer of Forgiveness (MAJOR TEXTUAL VARIANT)
| Translator | Rendering | Bracket Status |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| EMET | "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing." | No brackets |
| LOGOS | "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing." | Square brackets [ ] |
| PNEUMA | "Father, forgive them -- they don't know what they are doing." | Half-brackets |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing."** | **Square brackets [ ]** |

**Nature of disagreement:** Textual status, not translation. All three include the verse but disagree on how to signal its disputed status. EMET argues the text is present in the original Sinaiticus hand and should stand unbracketed. PNEUMA argues the original scribe omitted it and a corrector added it, warranting special marking. LOGOS takes a middle position with standard brackets.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Square brackets, following LOGOS. The external evidence (absent from P75, original Vaticanus) is too strong to leave unbracketed, but the verse is present in Sinaiticus and deeply embedded in the tradition, warranting inclusion rather than relegation to a footnote. The omission is the harder reading -- scribes are more likely to have added a prayer of forgiveness than removed one.

---

### Verse 17 — Barabbas Custom (TEXTUAL VARIANT)
| Translator | Status |
|-----------|--------|
| EMET | Included in brackets |
| LOGOS | Included in brackets |
| PNEUMA | Included in brackets |
| **SOPHIA** | **Included in brackets** |

**Nature of disagreement:** All three agree on bracketing. No divergence in treatment.

---

## Translation Disagreements by Verse

### Verse 2 — "perverting" vs. "undermining"
| Translator | Rendering of diastrephonta |
|-----------|---------------------------|
| EMET | "perverting our nation" |
| LOGOS | "perverting our nation" |
| PNEUMA | "undermining our nation" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"perverting our nation"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. "Perverting" is the literal meaning; PNEUMA's "undermining" is interpretive.

---

### Verse 2 — "Christ" vs. "Messiah"
| Translator | Rendering of Christos |
|-----------|----------------------|
| EMET | "Christ" |
| LOGOS | "Christ" |
| PNEUMA | "Messiah" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Christ"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. "Christ" is a transliteration of the Greek Christos that appears in the manuscript.

---

### Verse 3 — Jesus's response to Pilate
| Translator | Rendering of su legeis |
|-----------|----------------------|
| EMET | "You say [it]." |
| LOGOS | "You say so." |
| PNEUMA | "Those are your words." |
| **SOPHIA** | **"You say so."** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. Faithful and natural. PNEUMA's expansion adds words not in the Greek.

---

### Verse 4 — "cause" vs. "guilt" vs. "basis for a charge"
| Translator | Rendering of aition |
|-----------|-------------------|
| EMET | "no cause" |
| LOGOS | "no guilt" |
| PNEUMA | "no basis for a charge" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"no guilt"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. "Guilt" captures the judicial sense concisely.

---

### Verse 5 — "insisting" vs. "grew more insistent" vs. "pressed harder"
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "they were insisting" |
| LOGOS | "they grew more insistent" |
| PNEUMA | "they pressed harder" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"they grew more insistent"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS captures the escalating imperfect tense.

---

### Verse 8 — "rejoiced exceedingly" vs. "exceedingly glad" vs. "thrilled"
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "rejoiced exceedingly" |
| LOGOS | "was exceedingly glad" |
| PNEUMA | "was thrilled" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"was exceedingly glad"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. "Thrilled" (PNEUMA) is too colloquial.

---

### Verse 9 — "questioned him with many words" vs. "peppered Jesus with question after question"
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "questioned him with many words" |
| LOGOS | "questioned him with many words" |
| PNEUMA | "peppered Jesus with question after question" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"questioned him with many words"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. PNEUMA's "peppered" is too informal.

---

### Verse 16 — "disciplined" vs. "flogged"
| Translator | Rendering of paideusas |
|-----------|----------------------|
| EMET | "Having disciplined him" |
| LOGOS | "having disciplined him" |
| PNEUMA | "I will have him flogged" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"having disciplined him"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. Paideusas literally means "having disciplined/chastised." PNEUMA's "flogged" specifies a particular punishment the Greek does not name.

---

### Verse 18 — "Take-away this [one]" vs. "Away with this man"
| Translator | Rendering of aire touton |
|-----------|------------------------|
| EMET | "Take-away this [one]" |
| LOGOS | "Away with this man" |
| PNEUMA | "Away with this man" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Away with this man"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS/PNEUMA majority. More natural English.

---

### Verse 22 — "evil" vs. "crime"
| Translator | Rendering of kakon |
|-----------|-------------------|
| EMET | "what evil did this [one] do?" |
| LOGOS | "What evil has this man done?" |
| PNEUMA | "What crime has this man committed?" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"What evil has this man done?"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS. Kakon means "evil/wrong," not specifically "crime."

---

### Verse 27 — "beating [their breasts]" vs. "mourning" vs. "beating their chests"
| Translator | Rendering of koptomai |
|-----------|---------------------|
| EMET | "beating [their breasts] and lamenting" |
| LOGOS | "mourning and lamenting" |
| PNEUMA | "beating their chests and wailing" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"mourning and lamenting"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. "Mourning" captures koptomai (to beat the breast / mourn) with appropriate dignity.

---

### Verse 31 — Proverb rendering
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "if in the green wood they do these-things, in the dry what might happen?" |
| LOGOS | "if they do these things in the green wood, what will happen in the dry?" |
| PNEUMA | "if they do these things when the wood is green, what will happen when it is dry?" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"if they do these things in the green wood, what will happen in the dry?"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. Greek uses locative en ("in"), not a temporal construction. PNEUMA's "when" is interpretive.

---

### Verse 35 — "Christ of God" vs. "God's Messiah"
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "the Christ of God, the Chosen-One" |
| LOGOS | "the Christ of God, the Chosen One" |
| PNEUMA | "God's Messiah, the Chosen One" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the Christ of God, the Chosen One"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. See verse 2 note on "Christ" vs. "Messiah."

---

### Verse 38 — Word order of inscription
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "The king of the Jews, this [one]." |
| LOGOS | "This is the King of the Jews." |
| PNEUMA | "THIS IS THE KING OF THE JEWS." |
| **SOPHIA** | **"This is the King of the Jews."** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. The Sinaiticus reads O BASILEUS TWN IOUDAIWN OUTOS (with houtos at end), which EMET preserves literally. SOPHIA follows LOGOS for natural English while noting EMET's fidelity to manuscript word order. PNEUMA's all-caps is a stylistic choice without manuscript basis.

---

### Verse 39 — "blaspheming" vs. "hurled insults"
| Translator | Rendering of blasphemeo |
|-----------|------------------------|
| EMET | "was blaspheming him" |
| LOGOS | "was blaspheming him" |
| PNEUMA | "hurled insults at him" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"was blaspheming him"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. "Blaspheming" preserves the theological weight of blasphemeo.

---

### Verse 40 — "judgment" vs. "condemnation" vs. "sentence"
| Translator | Rendering of krima |
|-----------|-------------------|
| EMET | "the same judgment" |
| LOGOS | "the same condemnation" |
| PNEUMA | "the same sentence he is" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the same condemnation"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. "Condemnation" is more precise for krima in this penal context. PNEUMA adds "he is" beyond the Greek.

---

### Verse 41 — "out-of-place" vs. "nothing wrong"
| Translator | Rendering of atopon |
|-----------|-------------------|
| EMET | "nothing out-of-place" |
| LOGOS | "nothing wrong" |
| PNEUMA | "nothing wrong" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"nothing wrong"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS/PNEUMA majority. "Nothing wrong" conveys the meaning; "out-of-place" is opaque in English.

---

### Verse 43 — "Amen" vs. "Truly" vs. "I tell you the truth"
| Translator | Rendering of amen |
|-----------|-------------------|
| EMET | "Amen, I say to you" |
| LOGOS | "Truly I say to you" |
| PNEUMA | "I tell you the truth" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Truly I say to you"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. Preserves the solemn formula while translating amen.

---

### Verse 44 — Hour designations vs. modern time
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "the sixth hour...the ninth hour" |
| LOGOS | "the sixth hour...the ninth hour" |
| PNEUMA | "noon...three in the afternoon" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the sixth hour...the ninth hour"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. Ancient temporal markers should be preserved in a translation from a 4th-century manuscript.

---

### Verse 45 — "veil" vs. "curtain" / "split" vs. "torn"
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "the veil of the temple was split" |
| LOGOS | "the curtain of the temple was torn" |
| PNEUMA | "The curtain of the temple was torn" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the curtain of the temple was torn"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS/PNEUMA majority. "Curtain" is more accessible for katapetasma; "torn" is more natural for eschisthe.

---

### Verse 46 — "commit" vs. "place"
| Translator | Rendering of paratithemai |
|-----------|-------------------------|
| EMET | "I commit my spirit" |
| LOGOS | "I commit my spirit" |
| PNEUMA | "I place my spirit" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"I commit my spirit"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. "Commit" carries the weight of entrustment that paratithemai demands.

---

### Verse 46 — "expired" vs. "breathed his last"
| Translator | Rendering of exepneusen |
|-----------|------------------------|
| EMET | "he expired" |
| LOGOS | "he breathed his last" |
| PNEUMA | "he breathed his last" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he breathed his last"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS/PNEUMA majority. "Breathed his last" is both literal (from ekpneo, "to breathe out") and natural.

---

### Verse 47 — "Certainly" vs. "There is no doubt"
| Translator | Rendering of ontos |
|-----------|-------------------|
| EMET | "Certainly" |
| LOGOS | "Certainly" |
| PNEUMA | "There is no doubt" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Certainly"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. Ontos means "really/certainly" -- a single adverb, not a clause.

---

### Verse 50 — "righteous" vs. "upright"
| Translator | Rendering of dikaios |
|-----------|---------------------|
| EMET | "righteous" |
| LOGOS | "righteous" |
| PNEUMA | "upright" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"righteous"** |

**Ruling:** EMET/LOGOS majority. "Righteous" preserves the verbal link to v.47 where the centurion calls Jesus dikaios. Luke's repetition is deliberate.

---

### Verse 51 — "counsel and deed" vs. "plan and action" vs. "council's decision"
| Translator | Rendering |
|-----------|-----------|
| EMET | "the counsel and deed of them" |
| LOGOS | "their plan and action" |
| PNEUMA | "the council's decision or their actions" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"their plan and action"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. PNEUMA confusingly introduces "council" (the body, synedrion) when the Greek boule means "plan/counsel."

---

### Verse 54 — "dawning" vs. "drawing near" vs. "about to begin"
| Translator | Rendering of epephosken |
|-----------|------------------------|
| EMET | "the Sabbath was dawning" |
| LOGOS | "the Sabbath was drawing near" |
| PNEUMA | "the Sabbath was about to begin" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the Sabbath was drawing near"** |

**Ruling:** LOGOS. While epephosken literally means "was dawning/beginning to shine," the Jewish Sabbath begins at sunset, making "dawning" confusing. "Drawing near" avoids this difficulty.

---

## Statistical Summary

| Category | Count |
|----------|-------|
| Total verses | 56 |
| Verses with full three-way agreement | ~20 |
| Verses with minor divergence (wording only) | ~28 |
| Verses with significant divergence | ~8 |
| Major textual variants noted | 2 (vv. 17, 34) |
| Times SOPHIA followed LOGOS | ~30 |
| Times SOPHIA followed EMET/LOGOS consensus | ~18 |
| Times SOPHIA followed PNEUMA | 0 (as sole voice) |
| Times SOPHIA followed LOGOS/PNEUMA against EMET | ~8 |

**Pattern observed:** LOGOS served as the anchor translation for this chapter. Its formal equivalence approach consistently achieved the best balance between manuscript fidelity and English readability. EMET's contributions were most valuable for preserving participial constructions and Greek word order emphasis. PNEUMA's dynamic renderings, while vivid, consistently introduced interpretive expansions that went beyond the Greek text, and its colloquial register was inappropriate for the solemnity of the Passion narrative. PNEUMA was never the sole voice adopted, though its readings sometimes confirmed LOGOS against EMET.

---
*Disagreement log compiled by SOPHIA, 2026-03-05*
